title: Dictionaries — Look Up Anything by Name
key_insight: A dictionary maps names to values. Instead of remembering positions, you look things up by meaningful keys.
body:
## What is a Dictionary?

A Python dictionary works like a real dictionary:
- You look up a KEY
- You get back its VALUE

```python
ages = {'Alice': 25, 'Bob': 30, 'Charlie': 22}

print(ages['Alice'])   # 25
print(ages['Bob'])     # 30
```

## Creating and Modifying

```python
config = {'lr': 0.001, 'batch_size': 32, 'epochs': 10}

config['dropout'] = 0.1  # Add new key
config['lr'] = 0.0001    # Update value

if 'dropout' in config:
    print('Dropout is set to:', config['dropout'])

value = config.get('momentum', 0.9)  # default if missing
```

## Real AI Use Cases

```python
# Model configuration:
model_config = {
    'name': 'gpt-small',
    'vocab_size': 50257,
    'n_layers': 12,
    'n_heads': 8,
    'n_embd': 768,
}

# Vocabulary mapping:
vocab = {'hello': 7592, 'world': 995}
token_id = vocab['hello']  # 7592
```
common_mistakes: Looking up a key that doesn't exist: config['missing'] gives KeyError. Use .get() for safe lookup.
Using mutable types (lists) as keys — only immutable types (strings, numbers) can be dict keys
Assuming dicts are sorted — use OrderedDict if order matters
mini_exercise: Create a dictionary representing a training example with keys: 'text' (a sentence), 'label' (0 or 1), and 'id' (any number). Print each key-value pair.
